preamble: The shift from the voiced postalveolar fricative [ʒ] (zh) to the voiced alveolar fricative [z] is a process of fronting or depalatalization. This change is frequently observed in historical sound changes, dialectal mergers, and early language development as the place of articulation moves from behind the alveolar ridge forward to the teeth.

examples.note: Widespread merger where retroflex/postalveolar fricatives are realized as alveolar sibilants.
In certain Polish dialects, historical postalveolar sounds shifted back to alveolar sibilants.

sources: Hock, H. H. (1991). Principles of Historical Linguistics.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
